result:
The thing about light is that you never notice it until it's gone. You sit in a room, afternoon sun filtering through the curtains, dust motes swimming in the beams like tiny worlds, and you think the light will always be there. It doesn't announce its departure. It just leaves, and suddenly you're sitting in the blue hour, wondering where the day went, wondering why you didn't pay attention while it was happening.

I think that's how most things work. Love. Health. The particular way your mother laughs at jokes she doesn't fully understand. The sound of your father's voice when he's reading something that moves him, how it drops to a register that seems to come from somewhere deeper than his throat. You don't mark these things. You don't hold them up to the light and say, this, this is what I will miss. You just let them pass through you like water through a sieve, and later, in the dark, you reach for them and find nothing but the shape of holes where they used to be.

My grandmother kept a garden. Not a decorative garden, not the kind with neat rows and labels and the deliberate architecture of cultivated beauty. Hers was a garden of necessity and excess, tomatoes climbing over cucumbers, squash invading the bean poles, flowers she never planted springing up between the vegetables like stubborn thoughts between sentences. She would walk out there in the morning with bare feet and a coffee cup and just stand among it all, not doing anything, just being present. I asked her once what she was doing and she said she was listening. Listening to what? To the garden growing. To the morning arriving. To herself breathing.

I didn't understand that then. I was ten, and standing still was a form of torture. The world was a machine that ran on motion, and I was its most willing operator. Run, climb, throw, catch, chase, sprint, go. Stillness was the space between verbs, the white margin around the text of action. It meant nothing. It was nothing. I had no ears for listening.

Now I am thirty-seven, and I stand in my own backyard with bare feet and a coffee cup, and I don't do anything, and I call it listening. I call it being present. I have become my grandmother, and I understand what she was doing all those mornings, and it was the hardest thing in the world. Not because of any physical labor, but because of the labor of resisting the machine. The machine wants you to run, and you have to refuse. You have to say, I will not participate in the velocity. I will stand here while the others race. I will let the morning come to me.

And it does. It always does. The light arrives whether you chase it or not. The tomatoes ripen on their own timeline. The squash does not ask your permission to climb the fence. Things happen at the speed of things, and your job is only to witness them. To not rush them. To not demand they happen faster or slower or differently than they happen. To let the sentence complete itself before you start editing.

We are so afraid of silence. We fill every gap with noise, every pause with planning, every margin with commentary. We have become terrified of the empty space, as if emptiness were a kind of death, as if the act of stopping meant the end of living. But the empty space is where the thing grows. The margin is where the meaning lives. The pause is where the music actually occurs. Without the rest between notes, you have only noise. Without the silence between words, you have only chatter. Without the stillness between breaths, you have only the mechanical repetition of lungs, and no life at all.

I think about the people I have lost. Not the ones who died, but the ones who drifted. The friendships that ended not in fire but in fog, the conversations that petered out not with a bang but with a shrug. I think about how I never said, this matters. I think about how I never said, I am paying attention. I think about how I let the light leave the room and never followed it to the window to see where it went.

There is a word I learned once in a poem: *apricity*. It means the warmth of the sun in winter. I think about that word often, the specific naming of a specific kind of light, a light that exists only in the context of cold, a warmth you feel only because the air around you is trying to take the heat from your skin. Apricity is the kind of warmth that makes you aware of what it's fighting against. It doesn't ask you to ignore the winter. It asks you to feel both things at once. The cold. The warmth. The way they coexist.

That is what I am trying to learn now. Not to choose between the dark and the light, but to stand in the threshold and feel both. Not to run from the grief, but to sit with it until it becomes a kind of companion, a strange and quiet friend who doesn't demand anything except acknowledgment. Not to chase happiness, but to let it arrive like the morning, on its own timeline, without my intervention, and to be there when it comes, standing in the yard with bare feet and a coffee cup, listening.

The garden doesn't need me to grow. The morning doesn't need me to break. The light doesn't need me to see it. But I need to be there. I need to witness. I need to say, I saw this. I was here. It mattered. Not because my presence made it matter, but because my presence was how I learned what mattering felt like. How I learned that the world was alive and so was I.

Tomorrow I will stand in the yard again. I will hold my coffee and I will listen to the squash climbing the fence, the tomatoes ripening in their own sweet time, the morning arriving without my permission or assistance. And I will try to remember that the thing about light is that it was never mine to keep. It was only mine to notice.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to write 1000 words of whatever comes to me. This is a creative free-writing exercise. I'll let my mind wander and write something authentic, raw, and interesting. Let me just start writing and see where it takes me.

I think I'll write something introspective and literary - maybe a piece that captures the strange quality of consciousness, attention, the modern condition. Something that feels genuine and not overly polished.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
